text: Chapter 27 (Downbeat-upbeat / quarter-note playing) teaches quarter-note playing and rhythmic economy as tools for musical expression and flexibility, emphasizing that less-is-more restraint allows breathing room and dialogue with the rhythm section. Core techniques: playing tunes feeling in four, two, or one beat per measure while tempo stays constant; collapsing the pulse at fast tempos to combat the eighth-note trap; using varied metronome placements (on 2 and 4, off-beats, or omitted entirely) to develop independence. Characteristic moves are pedagogical: using limitation as teacher (restricting to half notes and quarter notes; starting with accents and filling around them) and emphasizing relaxation as cognitive prerequisite to time mastery. Key terminology: feeling in one/two/four, in back of the time, on top of the time, eighth-note trap.
